task_prompt:
Please calculate the DCF value per share applying the midyear convention. Keep all other assumptions the same in the existing model.

Provide your answer right here, rounded to the nearest cent
rendered_trajectory:
[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes

<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions

<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</example>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</example>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks

<memory_item>
<description>
After applying midyear convention, confirm that only the discount exponents were changed and that the delivered numeric answer matches the required rounding and delivery format (nearest cent, no extra text).
</description>
<example>
Verify rounding: final numeric result rounded to exactly two decimal places; confirm no other model inputs were edited.
</example>
</memory_item>

---

[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
When using the code_execution_server_code_exec tool to run Python, always invoke as python3 -c followed by the code in double quotes, with statements separated by semicolons if on one line; bare code or python (without 3) causes shell interpretation or command-not-found errors.
</description>
<example>
python3 -c "r = 0.1; values = [1,2,3]; result = 0.0; for n in range(1,4): result += values[n-1] / (1 + r) ** n; print(result)"
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</example>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</example>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
The code execution environment has Python 3 available and requires the explicit python3 command; the plain python command is not available or leads to shell interpretation.
</description>
<example>
Invoke as python3 -c "code"; confirm with which python3 if needed.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
After applying midyear convention, confirm that only the discount exponents were changed and that the delivered numeric answer matches the required rounding and delivery format (nearest cent, no extra text).
</description>
<example>
Verify rounding: final numeric result rounded to exactly two decimal places; confirm no other model inputs were edited.
</example>
</memory_item>

<description>

def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)

</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</example>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</example>
</memory_item>

[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
When using the code_execution_server_code_exec tool to run Python, always invoke as python3 -c followed by the code in double quotes. Use semicolons for simple statements on one line; for multi-line code with indentation, embed newlines as \n within the string. Bare code or python (without 3) causes shell interpretation or command-not-found errors.
</description>
<example>
python3 -c "r = 0.0985; fcfs = [149.1387682, 144.3343349, 150.5161488, 157.7324231, 165.1306757, 172.7043214]; tv = 2596.867898; cash = 182.947; debt = 0; shares = 50.574641; discounted = 0.0;\nfor n in range(1,7):\n    df = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)\n    cf = fcfs[n-1] if n < 6 else fcfs[n-1] + tv\n    discounted += cf * df\nprint('New EV:', discounted)"
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a financial model task requires finding the value of an input parameter that produces a target output metric (such as a specific IRR or yield), implement a Python replica of the model's key calculations and use a numerical search method such as bisection to solve for the parameter.
</description>
<example>
def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When an LBO model task requires revising the offer premium to CNS holders or the maximum Term Loan B leverage, update only the affected entry valuation (new share price = current * (1 + premium)) and debt amount (TLB drawn = new max); preserve all operating forecasts, margins, working capital, capex, tax, exit multiple, and debt terms; then recalculate entry equity value, sponsor equity, debt repayment schedule, and exit equity to obtain the revised IRR and MoM.
</description>
<example>
premium = task_premium; max_tlb = task_max_tlb; entry_price = current_price * (1 + premium); tlb = max_tlb; recompute_sources_uses(); run_debt_schedule(); compute_exit_equity(); irr = ...; mom = ...
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</example>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
The code execution environment has Python 3 available and requires the explicit python3 command; the plain python command is not available or leads to shell interpretation.
</description>
<example>
Invoke as python3 -c "code"; confirm with which python3 if needed.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
After applying midyear convention, confirm that only the discount exponents were changed and that the delivered numeric answer matches the required rounding and delivery format (nearest cent, no extra text).
</description>
<example>
Verify rounding: final numeric result rounded to exactly two decimal places; confirm no other model inputs were edited.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task provides specific assumptions (WACC, terminal growth, exit multiple) that differ from the model's built-in values, override with the task-specified values for the calculation; do not use the spreadsheet's embedded rates.
</description>
<example>
Confirm WACC = task_WACC, g = task_g, multiple = task_multiple before computing terminal values or yields.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task specifies the precision for the answer (e.g., to one decimal place), round the final numeric results accordingly and deliver only the numbers in the requested format without extra text.
</description>
<example>
final_value = round(computed, 1); submit exactly as "x.x y.y" or per the required channel and format.
</sample>
</memory_item>

Provide the mean implied equity value for CNS from the Comparables and Precedents using 2025E EBITDA, as well as the implied equity value from the DCF and LBO using the base DCF and assumed offer price.

rendered_trajectory:
[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
When using the code_execution_server_code_exec tool to run Python, always invoke as python3 -c followed by the code in double quotes. Use semicolons for simple statements on one line; for multi-line code with indentation, embed newlines as \n within the string. Bare code or python (without 3) causes shell interpretation or command-not-found errors.
</description>
<example>
python3 -c "r = 0.0985; fcfs = [149.1387682, 144.3343349, 150.5161488, 157.7324231, 165.1306757, 172.7043214]; tv = 2596.867898; cash = 182.947; debt = 0; shares = 50.574641; discounted = 0.0;\nfor n in range(1,7):\n    df = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)\n    cf = fcfs[n-1] if n < 6 else fcfs[n-1] + tv\n    discounted += cf * df\nprint('New EV:', discounted)"
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a financial model task requires finding the value of an input parameter that produces a target output metric (such as a specific IRR or yield), implement a Python replica of the model's key calculations and use a numerical search method such as bisection to solve for the parameter.
</description>
<example>
def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When an LBO model task requires revising the offer premium to CNS holders or the maximum Term Loan B leverage, update only the affected entry valuation (new share price = current * (1 + premium)) and debt amount (TLB drawn = new max); preserve all operating forecasts, margins, working capital, capex, tax, exit multiple, and debt terms; then recalculate entry equity value, sponsor equity, debt repayment schedule, and exit equity to obtain the revised IRR and MoM.
</description>
<example>
premium = task_premium; max_tlb = task_max_tlb; entry_price = current_price * (1 + premium); tlb = max_tlb; recompute_sources_uses(); run_debt_schedule(); compute_exit_equity(); irr = ...; mom = ...
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a valuation task requires implied equity values from Comparables, Precedents, DCF, and LBO methods, compute each separately using the task-specified inputs (2025E EBITDA for comps and precedents; base DCF model and assumed offer price for DCF and LBO), then calculate the arithmetic mean of the four, round to one decimal place, and deliver all five numbers (four methods + mean) in $ million.
</description>
<example>
implied_comps = ...; implied_precedents = ...; implied_dcf = ...; implied_lbo = ...; mean = round((implied_comps + implied_precedents + implied_dcf + implied_lbo)/4, 1); submit "x.x y.y z.z w.w mean m.m"
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
The code execution environment has Python 3 available and requires the explicit python3 command; the plain python command is not available or leads to shell interpretation.
</description>
<example>
Invoke as python3 -c "code"; confirm with which python3 if needed.
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
After applying midyear convention, confirm that only the discount exponents were changed and that the delivered numeric answer matches the required rounding and delivery format (nearest cent, no extra text).
</description>
<example>
Verify rounding: final numeric result rounded to exactly two decimal places; confirm no other model inputs were edited.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task provides specific assumptions (WACC, terminal growth, exit multiple) that differ from the model's built-in values, override with the task-specified values for the calculation; do not use the spreadsheet's embedded rates.
</description>
<example>
Confirm WACC = task_WACC, g = task_g, multiple = task_multiple before computing terminal values or yields.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task specifies the precision for the answer (e.g., to one decimal place), round the final numeric results accordingly and deliver only the numbers in the requested format without extra text.
</description>
<example>
final_value = round(computed, 1); submit exactly as "x.x y.y" or per the required channel and format.
</sample>
</memory_item>

task_prompt:
Your task is to evaluate the impact of financing constraints on Project Vanguard's take-private economics and develop a revised LBO case reflecting a capped leverage scenario using the LBO model.

• Term Loan B (TLB): cap maximum proceeds at $1,250 MM
• Adjust Sponsor Equity so that Total Sources = Total Uses, maintaining a constant enterprise value (EV) at entry (excluding fees and cash to balance sheet).
• Exit Multiple: assume 2030E Exit Multiple of 18.5x

In the existing LBO model, I want you to compute the Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A). Label this calculation as: “Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A)”. Also, create a 2x2 sensitivity tables for Sponsor IRR (%). Set rows as: Premium to Current (15.0%, 25.0%) and columns as: Exit Multiple (17.5x, 18.5x). Populate the tables with recalculated IRR values based on the revised capital structure reflecting the Term Loan B cap.

Round the final results to one decimal place and keep the same formatting as the original sensitivity table.

a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable

and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a

  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you

  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a financial model task requires finding the value of an input parameter that produces a target output metric (such as a specific IRR or yield), implement a Python replica of the model's key calculations and use a numerical search method such as bisection to solve for the parameter.
</description>
<example>
def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

• Exit Multiple: assume 2030E Exit Multiple of 18.5x

Also, create a 2x2 sensitivity tables for Sponsor IRR (%). Set rows as: Premium to Current (15.0%, 25.0%) and columns as: Exit Multiple (17.5x, 18.5x). Populate the tables with recalculated IRR values based on the revised capital structure reflecting the Term Loan B cap.

Round the final results to one decimal place and keep the same formatting as the original sensitivity table.

[48] ASSISTANT (tool_call): code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"python3 -c \"\ncurrent_price = 59.6\nshares = 50.574641\ncash_existing = 182.947\nrefi_net = -132.947\ncash_bs = 50.0\ntrans_fee_rate = 0.02\nfin_fee_rate = 0.02\ntlb_cap = 1250.0\nexit_ebitda = 300.6482275\nfcfs = [121.443186,134.5053165,149.4445603,166.3114316,188.0165354]\ndef simulate_tlb_end(tlb_start, fcfs, mand_rate=0.01):\n    tlb = tlb_start\n    mand = tlb_start * mand_rate\n    for fcf in fcfs:\n        avail = fcf - mand\n        tlb -= (mand + avail)\n        if tlb < 0: tlb = 0\n    return round(tlb, 6)\n\ndef compute_irr(premium, exit_mult, n_years=5):\n    entry_price = current_price * (1 + premium)\n    entry_equity = entry_price * shares\n    trans_fee = entry_equity * trans_fee_rate\n    fin_fee = tlb_cap * fin_fee_rate\n    total_uses = entry_equity + refi_net + cash_bs + trans_fee + fin_fee\n    sponsor_equity = total_uses - tlb_cap\n    tlb_end = simulate_tlb_end(tlb_cap, fcfs)\n    net_debt_exit = tlb_end - 50\n    exit_ev = exit_ebitda * exit_mult\n    exit_equity = exit_ev - net_debt_exit\n    mom = exit_equity / sponsor_equity\n    irr = mom ** (1 / n_years) - 1\n    return round(irr * 100, 1), round(mom, 3), round(sponsor_equity, 2), round(exit_equity, 2)\n\npremiums = [0.15, 0.25]\nexit_mults = [17.5, 18.5]\nresults = {}\nfor p in premiums:\n    for m in exit_mults:\n        irr, mom, se, ee = compute_irr(p, m)\n        results[(p, m)] = irr\n        print(f'Premium {p*100:.1f}%, Exit {m}x: IRR {irr}%, MoM {mom}, Sponsor Eq {se}, Exit Eq {ee}')\n\nprint(results)\n\""}})

task_prompt:
Assume that CNS has been taken private as of the start of FY25E. The new PE owners have decided to reduce employee compensation by 50% of the value of stock-based compensation in the previous LBO forecast (when CNS was a public company).

Based on this, calculate the revised DCF per share valuation of CNS. Keep all assumptions the same per the DCF base case.

Provide your response right here, rounded to the nearest cent.

[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
When using the code_execution_server_code_exec tool to run Python, always invoke as python3 -c followed by the code in double quotes. Use semicolons for simple statements on one line; for multi-line code with indentation, embed newlines as \n within the string. Bare code or python (without 3) causes shell interpretation or command-not-found errors.
</description>
<example>
python3 -c "r = 0.0985; fcfs = [149.1387682, 144.3343349, 150.5161488, 157.7324231, 165.1306757, 172.7043214]; tv = 2596.867898; cash = 182.947; debt = 0; shares = 50.574641; discounted = 0.0;\nfor n in range(1,7):\n    df = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)\n    cf = fcfs[n-1] if n < 6 else fcfs[n-1] + tv\n    discounted += cf * df\nprint('New EV:', discounted)"
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a financial model task requires finding the value of an input parameter that produces a target output metric (such as a specific IRR or yield), implement a Python replica of the model's key calculations and use a numerical search method such as bisection to solve for the parameter.
</description>
<example>
def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When an LBO model task requires revising the offer premium to CNS holders or the maximum Term Loan B leverage, update only the affected entry valuation (new share price = current * (1 + premium)) and debt amount (TLB drawn = new max); preserve all operating forecasts, margins, working capital, capex, tax, exit multiple, and debt terms; then recalculate entry equity value, sponsor equity, debt repayment schedule, and exit equity to obtain the revised IRR and MoM.
</description>
<example>
premium = task_premium; max_tlb = task_max_tlb; entry_price = current_price * (1 + premium); tlb = max_tlb; recompute_sources_uses(); run_debt_schedule(); compute_exit_equity(); irr = ...; mom = ...
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a valuation task requires implied equity values from Comparables, Precedents, DCF, and LBO methods, compute each separately using the task-specified inputs (2025E EBITDA for comps and precedents; base DCF model and assumed offer price for DCF and LBO), then calculate the arithmetic mean of the four, round to one decimal place, and deliver all five numbers (four methods + mean) in $ million.
</description>
<example>
implied_comps = ...; implied_precedents = ...; implied_dcf = ...; implied_lbo = ...; mean = round((implied_comps + implied_precedents + implied_dcf + implied_lbo)/4, 1); submit "x.x y.y z.z w.w mean m.m"
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When an LBO task requires building sensitivity tables for Sponsor IRR under different offer premiums and exit multiples while respecting a TLB cap, vary the premium (to recompute entry price, equity value, and sponsor equity after enforcing the TLB maximum and balancing sources/uses at constant entry EV) and exit multiple (to recompute exit EV and equity); for each combination run the debt schedule and derive IRR; present results in a 2x2 table (rows = premiums, columns = exit multiples) rounded to one decimal place.
</description>
<example>
For premiums 15.0%, 25.0% and exit multiples 17.5x, 18.5x: compute the four IRR values from the capped-TLB structure and format as table.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires revising an operating expense assumption such as employee compensation expense, extract the base compensation values from the model, apply the specified reduction (e.g., 50% of a referenced stock-based compensation value), update the NOPAT and Unlevered FCF calculations for the affected years, then recompute the enterprise value and per-share value using the standard DCF discounting and terminal value methods while preserving all other inputs including WACC, terminal growth, and shares.
</description>
<example>
compensation_reduction = 0.5 * stock_based_comp_value; updated_comp = base_comp - compensation_reduction; updated_nopat = base_nopat - (compensation_reduction * (1 - tax_rate)); updated_fcf = ...; then run standard DCF code with updated_fcf
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
The code execution environment has Python 3 available and requires the explicit python3 command; the plain python command is not available or leads to shell interpretation.
</description>
<example>
Invoke as python3 -c "code"; confirm with which python3 if needed.
</sample>
</memory_item>

</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task provides specific assumptions (WACC, terminal growth, exit multiple) that differ from the model's built-in values, override with the task-specified values for the calculation; do not use the spreadsheet's embedded rates.
</description>
<example>
Confirm WACC = task_WACC, g = task_g, multiple = task_multiple before computing terminal values or yields.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task specifies the precision for the answer (e.g., to one decimal place), round the final numeric results accordingly and deliver only the numbers in the requested format without extra text.
</description>
<example>
final_value = round(computed, 1); submit exactly as "x.x y.y" or per the required channel and format.
</sample>
</memory_item>

task_prompt:
Using the precedents and DCF analysis calculate an implied share price for CNS to 2 decimal places. Exclude transactions where the target had <$90 B in AUM. Using the forward EBITDA from the DCF, assume a 10% increase in total expenses and only a 5% increase in Depreciation and amortization. 

Edit the existing sheet to provide the share price build (EV / EBITDA, 2025E EBITDA, Implied EV, Net Debt, Implied Market Cap, FDSO and share price), starting in the 'Precedents' tab. Round all values to 2 decimal places.

<memory_item>
<description>
When a valuation task requires an implied share price from filtered precedents (AUM >= $90B) combined with adjusted DCF, filter the precedents table to retain only transactions meeting the AUM threshold, extract the mean or median EV/EBITDA multiple, adjust the DCF forward (2025E) total expenses upward by 10% and D&A by 5% to obtain the adjusted EBITDA, compute Implied EV = multiple * adjusted_EBITDA, Implied Market Cap = Implied EV - Net Debt, and share price = Implied Market Cap / FDSO; round all values to 2 decimal places and edit the 'Precedents' tab to insert the share price build starting with the columns EV/EBITDA, 2025E EBITDA, Implied EV, Net Debt, Implied Market Cap, FDSO, share price.
</description>
<example>
Filter precedents AUM >=90; multiple = mean_or_median; adjusted_ebitda = base_ebitda_with_expense_adj; ev = multiple * adjusted_ebitda; mkt_cap = ev - net_debt; price = round(mkt_cap / fdso, 2); edit 'Precedents' tab with build columns.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
The code execution environment has Python 3 available and requires the explicit python3 command; the plain python command is not available or leads to shell interpretation.
</description>
<example>
Invoke as python3 -c "code"; confirm with which python3 if needed.
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
After applying midyear convention, confirm that only the discount exponents were changed and that the delivered numeric answer matches the required rounding and delivery format (nearest cent, no extra text).
</description>
<example>
Verify rounding: final numeric result rounded to exactly two decimal places; confirm no other model inputs were edited.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task provides specific assumptions (WACC, terminal growth, exit multiple) that differ from the model's built-in values, override with the task-specified values for the calculation; do not use the spreadsheet's embedded rates.
</description>
<example>
Confirm WACC = task_WACC, g = task_g, multiple = task_multiple before computing terminal values or yields.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When the task specifies the precision for the answer (e.g., to one decimal place), round the final numeric results accordingly and deliver only the numbers in the requested format without extra text.
</description>
<example>
final_value = round(computed, 1); submit exactly as "x.x y.y" or per the required channel and format.
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When filtering precedents for AUM threshold and applying specified expense adjustments before computing implied share price, confirm only transactions with AUM >= $90B are retained, only total expenses (+10%) and D&A (+5%) are increased on the DCF forward figures, the share price build is placed starting in the 'Precedents' tab, and all values are rounded to exactly two decimal places.
</description>
<example>
Verify AUM filter excludes <$90B; confirm expense and D&A adjustments only; check build columns in 'Precedents' tab; round all to 2 decimals.
</sample>
</memory_item>

Can we see what the DCF per share value is if the terminal value is based on EV / AUM of the peer set? 
Let's exclude AMG and JHG for purposes of this exercise. Assume that AUM grows linearly with management fees, round to the nearest cent.
Print your answer back here.

Use semicolons for simple statements on one line; for multi-line code with indentation, embed newlines as \n within the string. Bare code or python (without 3) causes shell interpretation or command-not-found errors.
</description>

python3 -c "r = 0.0985; fcfs = [149.1387682, 144.3343349, 150.5161488, 157.7324231, 165.1306757, 172.7043214]; tv = 2596.867898; cash = 182.947; debt = 0; shares = 50.574641; discounted = 0.0;\nfor n in range(1,7):\n    df = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)\n    cf = fcfs[n-1] if n < 6 else fcfs[n-1] + tv\n    discounted += cf * df\nprint('New EV:', discounted)"

</description>

Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.

def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>

compensation_reduction = 0.5 * stock_based_comp_value; updated_comp = base_comp - compensation_reduction; updated_nopat = base_nopat - (compensation_reduction * (1 - tax_rate)); updated_fcf = ...; then run standard DCF code with updated_fcf

</description>
<example>
Filter precedents AUM >=90; multiple = mean_or_median; adjusted_ebitda = base_ebitda_with_expense_adj; ev = multiple * adjusted_ebitda; mkt_cap = ev - net_debt; price = round(mkt_cap / fdso, 2); edit 'Precedents' tab with build columns.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires the terminal value based on an EV/AUM multiple drawn from a peer set (with specified peers excluded), extract the relevant EV/AUM multiples from the peer data, compute the mean or median multiple, project forward AUM using the task-specified growth relation (such as linear with management fees), compute TV = multiple * projected_AUM, then integrate into the standard DCF discounting of FCFs plus TV to obtain enterprise value, equity value, and per-share value while preserving all other model inputs and rounding to the required precision.
</description>
<example>
multiple = mean_or_median(ev_aum_from_peers_excluding_specified); projected_aum = ...; tv = multiple * projected_aum; then discount FCFs + TV with standard WACC to get EV and per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
The code execution environment has Python 3 available and requires the explicit python3 command; the plain python command is not available or leads to shell interpretation.
</description>
<example>
Invoke as python3 -c "code"; confirm with which python3 if needed.
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>

task_prompt:
What is the EV and implied share price of CNS in the DCF model using both Gordon growth model and exit multiple approach if each business segment grows as outlined below over the projection period of 2025 to 2030?

Segment 1 - Investment advisory and administration fees grows at 7.0% revenue growth per annum
Segment 2 - Distribution and service fees grows at 6.0% revenue growth per annum
Segment 3 - Other grows at 5.0% revenue growth per annum

Output the following to me with a short message in reply:
1. EV using the Gordon growth method
2. Implied share price using the Gordon growth method
3. EV using the exit multiple approach
4. Implied share price using the exit multiple approach

Report share price in $ and to 2 decimal places, report EV in whole number and in millions. For operating expenses and capex use the Operating Assumptions (provided as a % of total revenue) laid out in the “LBO Model-hardcoded” tab.

        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

task_prompt:
Using a discounted cash flow (DCF) model, what is the implied share price under the following assumptions:
- Revenue growth is 2.0 percentage points lower than the current growth rates in each year from 2025 to 2030.
- Employee compensation and benefits as a percentage of revenue are 2.0 percentage points higher than the current figures in each year from 2025 to 2030.
- Mid-year convention is used.

I want you to (1) Tell me the implied share price using the Gordon Growth Method. Then, (2) tell me the implied share price using the Exit Multiple Approach. Reply to me with a message outlining these values in USD, rounded to two decimal places.

def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
When an existing DCF model must be updated to midyear convention while preserving all other inputs and assumptions, modify only the timing exponents used in the present-value calculations; do not alter revenues, margins, growth rates, capex, working capital, tax rates, or terminal-value parameters.
</description>
<example>
Locate the discounting formulas; replace each year-n end-of-year exponent with (n - 0.5); recompute the PV of each cash-flow line and the total enterprise value; divide by shares outstanding to obtain per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a financial model task requires finding the value of an input parameter that produces a target output metric (such as a specific IRR or yield), implement a Python replica of the model's key calculations and use a numerical search method such as bisection to solve for the parameter.
</description>
<example>
def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When an LBO model task requires revising the offer premium to CNS holders or the maximum Term Loan B leverage, update only the affected entry valuation (new share price = current * (1 + premium)) and debt amount (TLB drawn = new max); preserve all operating forecasts, margins, working capital, capex, tax, exit multiple, and debt terms; then recalculate entry equity value, sponsor equity, debt repayment schedule, and exit equity to obtain the revised IRR and MoM.
</description>

premium = task_premium; max_tlb = task_max_tlb; entry_price = current_price * (1 + premium); tlb = max_tlb; recompute_sources_uses(); run_debt_schedule(); compute_exit_equity(); irr = ...; mom = ...
</example>

</description>
<example>
implied_comps = ...; implied_precedents = ...; implied_dcf = ...; implied_lbo = ...; mean = round((implied_comps + implied_precedents + implied_dcf + implied_lbo)/4, 1); submit "x.x y.y z.z w.w mean m.m"

</description>
<example>
For premiums 15.0%, 25.0% and exit multiples 17.5x, 18.5x: compute the four IRR values from the capped-TLB structure and format as table.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires revising an operating expense assumption such as employee compensation expense, extract the base compensation values from the model, apply the specified reduction (e.g., 50% of a referenced stock-based compensation value), update the NOPAT and Unlevered FCF calculations for the affected years, then recompute the enterprise value and per-share value using the standard DCF discounting and terminal value methods while preserving all other inputs including WACC, terminal growth, and shares.
</description>
<example>
compensation_reduction = 0.5 * stock_based_comp_value; updated_comp = base_comp - compensation_reduction; updated_nopat = base_nopat - (compensation_reduction * (1 - tax_rate)); updated_fcf = ...; then run standard DCF code with updated_fcf
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a valuation task requires an implied share price from filtered precedents (AUM >= $90B) combined with adjusted DCF, filter the precedents table to retain only transactions meeting the AUM threshold, extract the mean or median EV/EBITDA multiple, adjust the DCF forward (2025E) total expenses upward by 10% and D&A by 5% to obtain the adjusted EBITDA, compute Implied EV = multiple * adjusted_EBITDA, Implied Market Cap = Implied EV - Net Debt, and share price = Implied Market Cap / FDSO; round all values to 2 decimal places and edit the 'Precedents' tab to insert the share price build starting with the columns EV/EBITDA, 2025E EBITDA, Implied EV, Net Debt, Implied Market Cap, FDSO, share price.
</description>
<example>
Filter precedents AUM >=90; multiple = mean_or_median; adjusted_ebitda = base_ebitda_with_expense_adj; ev = multiple * adjusted_ebitda; mkt_cap = ev - net_debt; price = round(mkt_cap / fdso, 2); edit 'Precedents' tab with build columns.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires revising revenue growth rates by business segment while preserving all other inputs, project each revenue segment independently using its specified growth rate to obtain forward revenues, compute total revenue, apply the operating expense and capex percentages of total revenue from the LBO Model-hardcoded tab to derive updated expenses and capex, recalculate NOPAT and unlevered FCF for each year, then recompute enterprise value and implied share price using the standard DCF discounting and terminal value methods (Gordon growth or exit multiple) while preserving WACC, tax rate, shares, and terminal parameters.
</description>
<example>
rev_seg1 = base_seg1 * (1 + g1)**n; rev_seg2 = base_seg2 * (1 + g2)**n; rev_seg3 = base_seg3 * (1 + g3)**n; total_rev = rev_seg1 + rev_seg2 + rev_seg3; opex = total_rev * pct_opex_from_lbo; capex = total_rev * pct_capex_from_lbo; ...; then run standard DCF with updated FCFs for both terminal methods.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires the terminal value based on an EV/AUM multiple drawn from a peer set (with specified peers excluded), extract the relevant EV/AUM multiples from the peer data, compute the mean or median multiple, project forward AUM using the task-specified growth relation (such as linear with management fees), compute TV = multiple * projected_AUM, then integrate into the standard DCF discounting of FCFs plus TV to obtain enterprise value, equity value, and per-share value while preserving all other model inputs and rounding to the required precision.
</description>
<example>
multiple = mean_or_median(ev_aum_from_peers_excluding_specified); projected_aum = ...; tv = multiple * projected_aum; then discount FCFs + TV with standard WACC to get EV and per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires simultaneously lowering all revenue growth rates by a fixed number of percentage points and raising employee compensation as a percentage of revenue by a fixed number of percentage points while also applying midyear convention, implement the combined adjustments in a Python replica of the model: subtract the pp shift from each original growth rate to project revenues, add the pp shift to the compensation pct to derive updated opex and NOPAT, compute the resulting FCF series, discount using (n-0.5) exponents, and obtain per-share values separately for Gordon growth and exit-multiple terminal methods while preserving WACC, tax rate, shares, and other inputs.
</description>
<example>
adjusted_g = base_g - delta_pp; adjusted_comp_pct = base_comp_pct + delta_pp; ... project revs and fcf with adjusted_g and adjusted_comp_pct; then discount with midyear df for both terminal methods to obtain the two share prices.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>

def solve_for_param(target_metric, low=0.0, high=0.2, tol=1e-6):
    for _ in range(50):
        mid = (low + high) / 2
        metric = compute_model_metric(mid)
        if metric < target_metric:
            low = mid
        else:
            high = mid
        if abs(metric - target_metric) < tol: break
    return round(mid, precision)
</example>

When a valuation task requires an implied share price from filtered precedents (AUM >= $90B) combined with adjusted DCF, filter the precedents table to retain only transactions meeting the AUM threshold, extract the mean or median EV/EBITDA multiple, adjust the DCF forward (2025E) total expenses upward by 10% and D&A by 5% to obtain the adjusted EBITDA, compute Implied EV = multiple * adjusted_EBITDA, Implied Market Cap = Implied EV - Net Debt, and share price = Implied Market Cap / FDSO; round all values to 2 decimal places and edit the 'Precedents' tab to insert the share price build starting with the columns EV/EBITDA, 2025E EBITDA, Implied EV, Net Debt, Implied Market Cap, FDSO, share price.
</description>
<example>
Filter precedents AUM >=90; multiple = mean_or_median; adjusted_ebitda = base_ebitda_with_expense_adj; ev = multiple * adjusted_ebitda; mkt_cap = ev - net_debt; price = round(mkt_cap / fdso, 2); edit 'Precedents' tab with build columns.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires revising revenue growth rates by business segment while preserving all other inputs, project each revenue segment independently using its specified growth rate to obtain forward revenues, compute total revenue, apply the operating expense and capex percentages of total revenue from the LBO Model-hardcoded tab to derive updated expenses and capex, recalculate NOPAT and unlevered FCF for each year, then recompute enterprise value and implied share price using the standard DCF discounting and terminal value methods (Gordon growth or exit multiple) while preserving WACC, tax rate, shares, and terminal parameters.
</description>
<example>
rev_seg1 = base_seg1 * (1 + g1)**n; rev_seg2 = base_seg2 * (1 + g2)**n; rev_seg3 = base_seg3 * (1 + g3)**n; total_rev = rev_seg1 + rev_seg2 + rev_seg3; opex = total_rev * pct_opex_from_lbo; capex = total_rev * pct_capex_from_lbo; ...; then run standard DCF with updated FCFs for both terminal methods.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires the terminal value based on an EV/AUM multiple drawn from a peer set (with specified peers excluded), extract the relevant EV/AUM multiples from the peer data, compute the mean or median multiple, project forward AUM using the task-specified growth relation (such as linear with management fees), compute TV = multiple * projected_AUM, then integrate into the standard DCF discounting of FCFs plus TV to obtain enterprise value, equity value, and per-share value while preserving all other model inputs and rounding to the required precision.
</description>
<example>
multiple = mean_or_median(ev_aum_from_peers_excluding_specified); projected_aum = ...; tv = multiple * projected_aum; then discount FCFs + TV with standard WACC to get EV and per-share value.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires simultaneously lowering all revenue growth rates by a fixed number of percentage points and raising employee compensation as a percentage of revenue by a fixed number of percentage points while also applying midyear convention, implement the combined adjustments in a Python replica of the model: subtract the pp shift from each original growth rate to project revenues, add the pp shift to the compensation pct to derive updated opex and NOPAT, compute the resulting FCF series, discount using (n-0.5) exponents, and obtain per-share values separately for Gordon growth and exit-multiple terminal methods while preserving WACC, tax rate, shares, and other inputs.
</description>
<example>
adjusted_g = base_g - delta_pp; adjusted_comp_pct = base_comp_pct + delta_pp; ... project revs and fcf with adjusted_g and adjusted_comp_pct; then discount with midyear df for both terminal methods to obtain the two share prices.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When a DCF task requires calculating the implied terminal growth rate consistent with a terminal value based on an EV / mgmt fees (or AUM) multiple from a peer set (with specified peers excluded), extract the EV / mgmt fees multiples from the peer data, compute the mean or median multiple, project the terminal mgmt fees using the task-specified growth relation (such as linear with management fees or revenue), compute the target TV = multiple * projected_mgmt_fees, then back out the implied g = WACC - FCF_{n+1} / TV and round the percentage to two decimal places while preserving all other model inputs and assumptions.
</description>

</example>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
Midyear convention treats each period's free cash flow as occurring at the midpoint of the year. For annual periods the discount factor for year n therefore uses the fractional exponent (n - 0.5).
</description>
<example>
DF_n = 1 / (1 + r) ** (n - 0.5)
</sample>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
The final per-share DCF value is the enterprise value (sum of discounted FCFs plus discounted terminal value) minus net debt, divided by the number of shares, then rounded to the nearest cent.
</description>
<example>
Value_per_share = round( (EV - Net_Debt) / Shares , 2 )
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
In LBO models, MoM (Multiple of Money) is exit equity value divided by sponsor equity invested. IRR is the compound annual return solving for the rate that equates sponsor cash flows, often approximated from MoM as (MoM ** (1 / holding_years)) - 1.
</description>
<example>
MoM = exit_equity / sponsor_equity
IRR_approx = MoM ** (1 / n_years) - 1
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied Adj. EBITDA Entry Multiple (2024A) is entry enterprise value divided by 2024A Adj. EBITDA.
</description>
<example>
Implied Entry Multiple = Entry_EV / 2024A_Adj_EBITDA
</sample>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Implied terminal growth rate g consistent with a target TV is given by g = WACC - FCF_{n+1} / TV.
</description>
<example>
g = r - FCF_{n+1} / TV
</sample>
</memory_item>
